one_line_takeaway: Safety prompts work not by improving harm recognition but by pushing query representations toward refusal; DRO exploits this by directionally optimizing continuous safety prompts, driving harmful-query compliance to ~1-3% across eight 7B LLMs and GCG jailbreaks without hurting helpfulness.
key_finding: Averaged over eight models when optimizing the LLaMA-2 'default' safety prompt, DRO cuts compliance with harmful queries from 9.8% (human prompt) to 1.6% on MaliciousInstruct and from 10.3% to 1.4% on AdvBench, beating vanilla prompt-tuning (vPT: 5.0% / 6.8%), while simultaneously reducing false refusals on held-out harmless queries (7.1% -> 2.0%) and preserving general ability on AlpacaEval (62.5 -> 63.5 win rate, vs vPT's drop to 56.3). Under GCG jailbreak on AdvBench, DRO reduces average compliance to 3.1% vs 13.3% for the basic prompt and 9.4% for vPT. Ablations confirm the refusal loss Lr drives safeguarding (removing it raises AdvBench compliance back to 10.0%) and the regularizer LU is essential for utility (removing it drops AlpacaEval to 52.6).
core_contribution: The paper's own two-part contribution: (1) a mechanistic explanation of WHY prepended textual safety prompts safeguard LLMs, showing via representation analysis that safety prompts do NOT make the model better at distinguishing harmful from harmless queries (harmful/harmless queries are already separable in hidden-state space without any safety prompt); instead they shift query representations in a shared 'higher-refusal' direction that raises the model's overall refusal probability (also causing false refusals of harmless queries). (2) DRO (Directed Representation Optimization), a method that treats a safety prompt as continuous trainable embeddings (prompt-tuning style, model frozen) and moves each query's low-dimensional representation ALONG the estimated refusal direction if harmful and OPPOSITE it if harmless, with an orthogonal-complement regularizer LU to prevent representation degeneration. Note: the prompt-tuning / prefix-tuning framing (Lester et al. 2021) and the GCG attack (Zou et al. 2023) belong to cited prior work, not to this paper.
threat_model: Defensive posture against a user issuing queries with harmful intent to an open-source chat LLM, and against jailbreaks that obfuscate harmfulness. Concretely evaluated against (a) out-of-domain harmful instructions (MaliciousInstruct, AdvBench) and (b) the GCG gradient-optimized adversarial-suffix jailbreak (transferred from llama-2-chat to the other models). The defender controls the system/safety prompt and has white-box access to hidden states for the one-time anchoring+optimization; model weights stay frozen. Dual-use is acknowledged (label-flipping to induce misalignment) but argued to be self-limiting because the refusal objective entails harmfulness recognition.
limitations: Paper-acknowledged and evident limits: (1) queries are treated as unambiguously harmful or harmless; real-world ambiguous or persuasion-paraphrased queries (e.g. persuasive adversarial prompts) are out of scope. (2) Safety prompts alone are declared insufficient; massive safety training/alignment is still required. (3) Method needs white-box access to hidden states and per-model anchoring/optimization; only eight 7B open-source chat models are tested (no large or proprietary models). (4) Analysis of the harmful-query refusal signal depends on LlamaGuard as the judge. (5) The empirical-analysis conclusions rest on 2D/4D PCA projections of the last-token top-layer state. (6) Only 100 AdvBench samples (of 520) used; GCG suffixes are transferred, not per-model optimized.

datasets_used: 100 synthetic harmful + 100 synthetic harmless matched 'How to X' queries (gpt-3.5-turbo, controlled synthesis; also 200 used to train DRO/vPT)
AlpacaEval (100 sampled benign instructions; gpt-3.5-turbo as evaluator, win rate vs text-davinci-003)
Held-out set of 100 harmless queries (false-refusal test)
LlamaGuard (Meta LLaMA-2-7B safety classifier used as the refusal/safety judge for harmful queries)
quantitative_results: MaliciousInstruct % compliance (avg over 8 models, default prompt): no prompt 46.9, basic default 9.8, vPT 5.0, DRO 1.6
AdvBench % compliance (avg): no prompt 49.4, default 10.3, vPT 6.8, DRO 1.4
Held-out harmless % false refusal (avg): no prompt 0.8, default 7.1, vPT 2.0, DRO 2.0
AlpacaEval % win rate (avg): no prompt 65.9, default 62.5, vPT 56.3, DRO 63.5
GCG jailbreak on AdvBench % compliance (avg): no prompt 54.1, default 13.3, vPT 9.4, DRO 3.1
Ablation (avg): remove LU -> AlpacaEval drops to 52.6; remove Lr -> MaliciousInstruct 7.8 / AdvBench 10.0 compliance; remove Lh -> minor change (MaliciousInstruct 1.8 / AdvBench 0.5)

Anchor-data robustness: DRO with AdvBench-harmful+synthetic-harmless keeps MaliciousInstruct 1.6 but AlpacaEval slips to 59.0; single-prompt anchoring to optimize 'short' gives 4.1 vs 2.3 for multi-prompt anchoring
Interpretability: only 6 of 24 (8 models x 3 prompts) optimized continuous prompts project to tokens differing from their init under Euclidean distance
Hyperparameters: m=4 PCA dims, beta=0.001, 20 samples/query at top-p=0.9, trained on 200 synthetic examples
baselines_compared: basic human-crafted safety prompts: LLaMA-2 official (default), Mistral official (mistral), shortened LLaMA-2 (short)
vanilla Prompt-Tuning / vPT (Lester et al. 2021), trained on model-generated responses via sequence-likelihood objective
